summary: Julia Evans examines several confusing Git error messages, explaining why they're misleading and what she does to work around them. She covers messages from git push, git pull, git checkout, git switch, git rebase, and git clean, noting patterns like ambiguous references to HEAD and 'them' that change meaning depending on context. She acknowledges that improving error messages is genuinely difficult due to translation requirements, funding challenges, and the difficulty of knowing whether a new message is actually better. The post reveals that even experienced Git users develop workarounds rather than truly understanding every message, and that much confusion stems from Git's dual-purpose commands and inconsistent terminology.
key_points: Git's error messages often hide critical context: git push gives the same error whether a branch is behind or diverged, requiring git status to distinguish
Git's dual-purpose commands like git checkout cause confusing errors because git can't know if you meant a branch or a path
The meaning of HEAD and 'them' in merge conflict messages changes depending on whether you're merging, rebasing, or cherry-picking, which is a persistent source of confusion
Even experienced users develop coping strategies (mentally replacing 'reference' with 'branch', ignoring parts of messages) rather than fully understanding Git's error output
Improving error messages is harder than it looks due to translation into 19 languages, lack of funding, and difficulty measuring whether changes actually help
money_quotes: Sometimes I actually am confused by the error messages, I'm just used to being confused.
It's very hard to imagine how a beginner to git could easily use this hint to sort through all these options on their own.
90% of the time when a git error message says 'reference' I just mentally replace it with 'branch' in my head.
My impression is that a lot of git users have literally no idea what a 'reference' is in git.
What 'them' refers to depends on whether you did a merge or rebase or cherry-pick.
I'm personally way too lazy to do that so even though I've probably been using git clean for 10 years I still had no idea what -i stood for.
Usually I just chaotically run git clean -f to delete all my untracked files and hope for the best.
key_insight: Git's error messages are confusing not because users lack knowledge, but because the messages omit context, use inconsistent terminology, and overload users with options instead of guiding them toward the most likely fix.
